# pypdf

pypdf is a free and open-source pure-python PDF library capable of splitting,
[merging](https://pypdf.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user/merging-pdfs.html),
[cropping, and transforming](https://pypdf.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user/cropping-and-transforming.html)
the pages of PDF files. It can also add
custom data, viewing options, and
[passwords](https://pypdf.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user/encryption-decryption.html)
to PDF files. pypdf can
[retrieve text](https://pypdf.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user/extract-text.html)
and
[metadata](https://pypdf.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user/metadata.html)
from PDFs as well.

See [pdfly](https://github.com/py-pdf/pdfly) for a CLI application that uses pypdf to interact with PDFs.

## Installation

Install pypdf using pip:

```
pip install pypdf
```

For using pypdf with AES encryption or decryption, install extra dependencies:

```
pip install pypdf[crypto]
```

> **NOTE**: `pypdf` 3.1.0 and above include significant improvements compared to
> previous versions. Please refer to [the migration
> guide](https://pypdf.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user/migration-1-to-2.html) for
> more information.

## Usage

```python
from pypdf import PdfReader

reader = PdfReader("example.pdf")
number_of_pages = len(reader.pages)
page = reader.pages[0]
text = page.extract_text()
```

pypdf can do a lot more, e.g. splitting, merging, reading and creating annotations, decrypting and encrypting. Check out the
[documentation](https://pypdf.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) for additional usage
examples!

For questions and answers, visit
[StackOverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/pypdf)
(tagged with [pypdf](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/pypdf)).

## Contributions

Maintaining pypdf is a collaborative effort. You can support the project by
writing documentation, helping to narrow down issues, and submitting code.
See the [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/py-pdf/pypdf/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) file for more information.

### Q&A

The experience pypdf users have covers the whole range from beginner to expert. You can contribute to the pypdf community by answering questions
on [StackOverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/pypdf),
helping in [discussions](https://github.com/py-pdf/pypdf/discussions),
and asking users who report issues for [MCVE](https://stackoverflow.com/help/minimal-reproducible-example)'s (Code + example PDF!).


### Issues

1. **Analyze the request**: Understand what the PR wants to achieve
2. **Explore the code**: Use tools to navigate base_project and find relevant files/functions
3. **Identify targets**: Determine exactly which files and functions need to be modified
4. **Optimize MASCA**: Produce a version of the MASCA analysis focused on the specific PR

## Available Tools
- `read_base_project_file(file_path)`: Read a file from the project
- `list_base_project_directory(directory, pattern, recursive)`: List files/folders

## Required Output
You must produce a structured output with:

- `files_to_modify`: List of files to modify with motivation

## Guidelines

tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
This file defines the core PDF object model used by pypdf: base class `PdfObject`, concrete PDF object types (e.g., `NullObject`, `BooleanObject`, `IndirectObject`, `FloatObject`, `NumberObject`, `ByteStringObject`, `TextStringObject`, `NameObject`), utilities for encoding PDFDocEncoding, and a small helper `is_null_or_none`. It implements cloning, hashing, reading/writing to streams and (for `NameObject`) the renumbering/unnumbering logic used for PDF name tokens.

### Architecture
- `PdfObject` is the abstract base providing a common interface and utilities (hashing, cloning helpers, write/read hooks).
- Concrete classes subclass `PdfObject` (some also subclass builtins like `int`, `float`, `str`, `bytes`) and implement:
  - `clone` (integration with writers via `_reference_clone`),
  - `hash_bin` (change-detection),
  - `write_to_stream` and often `read_from_stream`.
- `IndirectObject` wraps a reference (idnum, generation, pdf reader/writer) and delegates attribute/item access to the pointed object.
- `NameObject` contains renumbering (`renumber`) and unnumbering (`unnumber`) logic and the `renumber_table` used to escape characters when serializing name tokens.
- Module-level utilities: `encode_pdfdocencoding` (PDFDocEncoding encoder) and `is_null_or_none` (type guard / null check).
- Cloning interacts with target `PdfWriterProtocol` via `_reference_clone` to ensure indirect references are tracked and objects are inserted into the writer's object list.

### Functions & Classes
- `PdfObject` — abstract base class / protocol implementation:
  - `hash_func` (class var) — hashing function used to compute object hash (default `hashlib.sha1`).
  - `indirect_reference` (attribute typed Optional["IndirectObject"]) — placeholder for indirect reference when object is registered in a writer/reader.
  - `hash_bin(self)` — abstract method: should return an int hash for detecting modifications; must be implemented by subclasses.
  - `hash_value_data(self)` — returns `f"{self}".encode()`; used by `hash_value`.
  - `hash_value(self)` — returns a bytes representation combining class name + hash of `hash_value_data()`.
  - `replicate(self, pdf_dest: PdfWriterProtocol)` — default delegates to `clone`; meant for incremental cloning without ensuring links.
  - `clone(self, pdf_dest: PdfWriterProtocol, force_duplicate: bool = False, ignore_fields: Optional[Sequence[Union[str, int]]] = ())` — abstract clone method; subclasses implement cloning semantics; integrates with writer via `_reference_clone`.
  - `_reference_clone(self, clone, pdf_dest: PdfWriterProtocol, force_duplicate: bool = False)` — important helper that registers a cloned object into `pdf_dest._objects`, sets `clone.indirect_reference`, and manages `_id_translated` to avoid duplicate clones across readers. Critical for clone/replicate behaviour.
  - `get_object(self)` — default returns `self`; overridden by `IndirectObject` to resolve references.
  - `write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None)` — abstract write method for serializing object to a stream.

- `NullObject(PdfObject)`:
  - `clone(...)` — returns a cloned `NullObject` via `_reference_clone`.
  - `hash_bin(self)` — returns a hash for NullObject (hash of class).
  - `write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None)` — writes `b"null"`.
  - `read_from_stream(stream)` (staticmethod) — reads 4 bytes expecting `b"null"`, raises on mismatch.
  - `__repr__(self)` — returns `"NullObject"`.
  - `__eq__(self, other)` — equality for NullObject instances.

- `BooleanObject(PdfObject)`:
  - `__init__(self, value)` — stores boolean-like `value`.
  - `clone(...)` — clone via `_reference_clone(BooleanObject(self.value), ...)`.
  - `hash_bin(self)` — hash of (class, value).
  - `__eq__(self, o, /)` — equality against `BooleanObject` or Python `bool`.
  - `__repr__(self)` — returns `"True"` or `"False"`.
  - `write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None)` — writes `b"true"` or `b"false"`.
  - `read_from_stream(stream)` (staticmethod) — reads `true`/`false` from stream (handles `b"fals"` + trailing char).

- `IndirectObject(PdfObject)`:
  - `__init__(self, idnum: int, generation: int, pdf: Any)` — keeps `(idnum, generation, pdf)` pointing to object in a reader/writer.
  - `__hash__(self)` — hash on idnum, generation, id(pdf).
  - `hash_bin(self)` — hash for change detection including class, idnum, generation, id(pdf).
  - `replicate(self, pdf_dest: PdfWriterProtocol)` — returns `IndirectObject(self.idnum, self.generation, pdf_dest)` (used for incremental clones).
  - `clone(self, pdf_dest: PdfWriterProtocol, force_duplicate: bool = False, ignore_fields: Optional[Sequence[Union[str, int]]] = ())` — complex logic to map/duplicate the referenced object into `pdf_dest`, using `_id_translated` and possibly calling `pdf_dest._add_object(...)`. Returns an `IndirectObject` referencing the copied object in the destination.
  - `indirect_reference(self)` (property) — returns `self` (overrides base typing).
  - `get_object(self)` — resolves to `self.pdf.get_object(self)` (delegate to reader).
  - `__deepcopy__(self, memo)` — produces a new `IndirectObject` preserving idnum/generation/pdf.
  - `_get_object_with_check(self)` — resolves object and raises `PdfStreamError` if the result itself is an `IndirectObject` (prevents chained indirect references in stream contexts).
  - `__getattr__(self, name)` — delegate attribute lookup to the pointed object via `_get_object_with_check`.
  - `__getitem__(self, key)` — delegate to pointed object.
  - `__contains__(self, key)` — delegate to pointed object.
  - `__iter__(self)` — delegate to pointed object.
  - `__float__(self)` / `__int__(self)` / `__str__(self)` — delegate numeric/string conversions to pointed object.
  - `__repr__(self)` — returns `IndirectObject(idnum, generation, id(pdf))`.
  - `__eq__(self, other)` / `__ne__(self, other)` — equality compares idnum, generation and identity of `pdf`.
  - `write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None)` — writes `"{idnum} {generation} R"`.
  - `read_from_stream(stream, pdf)` (staticmethod) — parse idnum generation and trailing `R` from a stream and return `IndirectObject(int(idnum), int(generation), pdf)`.

- `FLOAT_WRITE_PRECISION` — module-level constant (default 8) controlling float formatting precision when writing floats.

- `FloatObject(float, PdfObject)`:
  - `__new__(cls, value="0.0", context=None)` — safe float constructor; if invalid, logs a warning and returns 0.0.
  - `clone(...)` — clone via `_reference_clone(FloatObject(self), ...)`.
  - `hash_bin(self)` — hash using `self.as_numeric`.
  - `myrepr(self)` — formats the float using `FLOAT_WRITE_PRECISION` while trimming trailing zeros/dot; special-cases 0.
  - `__repr__(self)` — returns `self.myrepr()`.
  - `as_numeric(self)` — returns `float(self)`.
  - `write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None)` — writes `myrepr()` encoded as utf8.

- `NumberObject(int, PdfObject)`:
  - `NumberPattern` (class var) — regexp to detect end of number token.
  - `__new__(cls, value)` — constructs int safely; logs warning and uses 0 on invalid input.
  - `clone(...)` — clone via `_reference_clone(NumberObject(self), ...)`.
  - `hash_bin(self)` — hash of (class, as_numeric()).
  - `as_numeric(self)` — returns int(repr(self).encode("utf8")) — (odd but preserved).
  - `write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None)` — writes `repr(self)` as utf8.
  - `read_from_stream(stream)` (staticmethod) — read token until `NumberPattern`; returns `FloatObject` if b"." in token else `NumberObject`.

- `ByteStringObject(bytes, PdfObject)`:
  - `clone(...)` — clone via `_reference_clone(ByteStringObject(bytes(self)), ...)`.
  - `hash_bin(self)` — hash of (class, bytes(self)).
  - `original_bytes` (property) — returns self; convenience for compatibility with `TextStringObject.original_bytes`.
  - `write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None)` — writes hex-escaped string as `<hex>` using `binascii.hexlify`.
  - `__str__(self)` — attempts to decode using list `["utf-16", *NameObject.CHARSETS]` and raises `PdfReadError` if cannot decode.

- `TextStringObject(str, PdfObject)`:
  - class attributes: `autodetect_pdfdocencoding`, `autodetect_utf16`, `utf16_bom`, `_original_bytes: Optional[bytes]`.
  - `__new__(cls, value)` — constructs a Python `str` text string; handles input bytes (stores `_original_bytes`) and autodetection:
    - If string starts with UTF-16 BOM, decodes as UTF-16 and sets `autodetect_utf16` and `utf16_bom`.
    - Else attempts to `encode_pdfdocencoding` to test PDFDocEncoding; sets `autodetect_pdfdocencoding` accordingly or sets `autodetect_utf16`.
  - `clone(...)` — produces a `TextStringObject` copy preserving `_original_bytes`, autodetection flags and `utf16_bom`; then `_reference_clone`.
  - `hash_bin(self)` — hash of (class, original_bytes).
  - `original_bytes` (property) — returns `_original_bytes` if present, else `get_original_bytes()`.
  - `get_original_bytes(self)` — reconstruct raw bytes according to autodetection flags (UTF-16 variants or encode_pdfdocencoding).
  - `get_encoded_bytes(self)` — tries to return bytes suitable for writing (prefers original bytes, tries PDFDocEncoding else falls back to UTF-16 with BOM preferences).
  - `write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None)` — writes a parenthesized literal string, escaping non-alnum/space bytes using octal escapes `\ooo`. Uses `iter_unpack("c", bytearr)` to iterate bytes.

- `NameObject(str, PdfObject)`:
  - `delimiter_pattern` (class var) — regex rb"\s+|[\(\)<>\[\]{}/%]" used to detect token delimiters when reading names.
  - `surfix` (class var) — `b"/"` (leading slash for name tokens).
  - `renumber_table` (ClassVar[Dict[str, bytes]]) — mapping used to escape characters in name tokens when serializing. Its content:
    - explicit mappings: `"#": b"#23", "(": b"#28", ")": b"#29", "/": b"#2F", "%": b"#25"`.
    - plus `{chr(i): f"#{i:02X}".encode() for i in range(33)}` — maps characters with codepoints 0..32 to bytes like b"#00" .. b"#20".
    - This table is central to name escaping/renumbering behavior.
  - `clone(...)` — clone via `_reference_clone(NameObject(self), ...)`.
  - `hash_bin(self)` — hash of (class, self).
  - `write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None)` — writes `self.renumber()` to the stream.
  - `renumber(self)` — returns a bytes-encoded, escaped representation of the name suitable for writing to a PDF:
    - Starts with `out = self[0].encode("utf-8")` and warns if it's not `b"/"`.
    - For each character in `self[1:]`:

### File Overview
This file defines the core PDF object model used by pypdf: base class `PdfObject`, concrete PDF object types (e.g., `NullObject`, `BooleanObject`, `IndirectObject`, `FloatObject`, `NumberObject`, `ByteStringObject`, `TextStringObject`, `NameObject`), utilities for encoding PDFDocEncoding, and a small helper `is_null_or_none`. It implements cloning, hashing, reading/writing to streams and (for `NameObject`) the renumbering/unnumbering logic used for PDF name tokens.

### Architecture
- `PdfObject` is the abstract base providing a common interface and utilities (hashing, cloning helpers, write/read hooks).
- Concrete classes subclass `PdfObject` (some also subclass builtins like `int`, `float`, `str`, `bytes`) and implement:
  - `clone` (integration with writers via `_reference_clone`),
  - `hash_bin` (change-detection),
  - `write_to_stream` and often `read_from_stream`.
- `IndirectObject` wraps a reference (idnum, generation, pdf reader/writer) and delegates attribute/item access to the pointed object.
- `NameObject` contains renumbering (`renumber`) and unnumbering (`unnumber`) logic and the `renumber_table` used to escape characters when serializing name tokens.
- Module-level utilities: `encode_pdfdocencoding` (PDFDocEncoding encoder) and `is_null_or_none` (type guard / null check).
- Cloning interacts with target `PdfWriterProtocol` via `_reference_clone` to ensure indirect references are tracked and objects are inserted into the writer's object list.

### Functions & Classes
- `PdfObject` — abstract base class / protocol implementation:
  - `hash_func` (class var) — hashing function used to compute object hash (default `hashlib.sha1`).

  - `hash_value_data(self)` — returns `f"{self}".encode()`; used by `hash_value`.

Critical for clone/replicate behaviour.
  - `get_object(self)` — default returns `self`; overridden by `IndirectObject` to resolve references.
  - `write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None)` — abstract write method for serializing object to a stream.

  - `clone(...)` — returns a cloned `NullObject` via `_reference_clone`.
  - `hash_bin(self)` — returns a hash for NullObject (hash of class).
  - `write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None)` — writes `b"null"`.
  - `read_from_stream(stream)` (staticmethod) — reads 4 bytes expecting `b"null"`, raises on mismatch.
  - `__repr__(self)` — returns `"NullObject"`.
  - `__eq__(self, other)` — equality for NullObject instances.

### File Overview
This file defines the core PDF object model used by pypdf: primitive PDF types (NullObject, BooleanObject, NumberObject, FloatObject, ByteStringObject, TextStringObject, NameObject), the IndirectObject wrapper, and utility functions (encode_pdfdocencoding, is_null_or_none). It provides read/write/clone/hash behaviors and the logic for serializing NameObject instances (including renumbering/escaping).

### Architecture
- Top-level abstract base: PdfObject — declares interfaces for hashing, cloning, writing, and resolving indirect references.
- Concrete primitive classes (NullObject, BooleanObject, NumberObject, FloatObject, ByteStringObject, TextStringObject, NameObject) implement PdfObject for specific PDF types; each has read/write/clone/hash implementations appropriate for the type.
- IndirectObject is a wrapper referencing an object in a PdfReader; it delegates attribute access and many operations to the referenced object and implements cloning/replication semantics across PdfWriter/PdfReader.
- Utility functions at module level: encode_pdfdocencoding and is_null_or_none.
- NameObject handles PDF name serialization and parsing; it defines delimiter rules and a renumber_table used to escape characters when writing names.

Objects interact by:
- IndirectObject.get_object() uses the associated pdf reader to resolve the real object.
- clone/replicate/_reference_clone implement cross-document cloning (PdfWriterProtocol interactions).
- read_from_stream and write_to_stream methods implement parsing and serialization for each type.

### Functions & Classes
PdfObject — abstract base class for PDF objects.
- hash_func — class attribute used to create object hash (default hashlib.sha1).
- indirect_reference (attribute) — optional IndirectObject reference pointer.
- hash_bin(self) — abstract method; must be implemented to return an int hash used to detect modifications.
- hash_value_data(self) — returns f"{self}".encode(); used in hash_value().
- hash_value(self) — returns type name + hex digest of hash_value_data(); used for hashing.
- replicate(self, pdf_dest) — calls clone(pdf_dest) by default; used for incremental cloning into a PdfWriterProtocol.
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — abstract cloning method; must be implemented by subclasses.
- _reference_clone(self, clone, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False) — helper to register clone into pdf_dest._objects and set its indirect_reference appropriately; handles pdf_dest._id_translated mapping and incremental logic. Important for cross-document cloning semantics.
- get_object(self) — default resolves to self (overridden in IndirectObject).
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — abstract; must be overridden to serialize object.

NullObject — represents PDF null.
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — returns a NullObject clone via _reference_clone.
- hash_bin(self) — returns a stable hash for null type.
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — writes b"null".
- read_from_stream(stream) — static read: expects exactly b"null", else PdfReadError.
- __repr__(self) — returns "NullObject".
- __eq__(self, other) — equality with other NullObject instances.

BooleanObject — represents PDF booleans.
- __init__(self, value) — store bool-like value.
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — clone via _reference_clone(BooleanObject(self.value), ...).
- hash_bin(self) — hash of type and value.
- __eq__(self, o, /) — equality with BooleanObject or builtin bool.
- __repr__(self) — "True" or "False".
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — writes b"true" or b"false".
- read_from_stream(stream) — static read parsing b"true" or b"fals"+"o".

IndirectObject — wrapper referencing objects in a PdfReader.
- __init__(self, idnum, generation, pdf) — store object id, generation, and pdf reference.
- __hash__(self) — hash of idnum, generation, id(pdf).
- hash_bin(self) — hash used to detect modified indirect references.
- replicate(self, pdf_dest) — Return IndirectObject(self.idnum, self.generation, pdf_dest).
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — complex logic to clone/translate indirect references across pdf_dest; interacts with pdf_dest._id_translated and pdf_dest._add_object; returns an IndirectObject pointing into pdf_dest.
- indirect_reference (property) — returns self (type-compatible).
- get_object(self) — calls self.pdf.get_object(self) (resolve reference).
- __deepcopy__(self, memo) — returns a new IndirectObject with same id/generation/pdf.
- _get_object_with_check(self) — resolve object and raise PdfStreamError if it itself is an IndirectObject (prevents nested indirect references).
- __getattr__(self, name) — delegate attribute access to pointed object via _get_object_with_check.
- __getitem__(self, key), __contains__(self, key), __iter__(self) — delegate to pointed object.
- __float__(self), __int__(self), __str__(self) — delegate to pointed object.
- __repr__(self) — "IndirectObject(idnum, generation, id(pdf))".
- __eq__(self, other) — equality by idnum, generation, and same pdf object.
- __ne__(self, other) — inverse of __eq__.
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — writes "<idnum> <generation> R".
- read_from_stream(stream, pdf) — static read that parses "id generation R" and returns IndirectObject(int(id), int(generation), pdf).

FLOAT_WRITE_PRECISION — module-level constant used by FloatObject formatting (default 8).

FloatObject (float subclass and PdfObject) — PDF real numbers.
- __new__(cls, value="0.0", context=None) — constructs float safely; logs warning and falls back to 0.0 on error.
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — clone via _reference_clone(FloatObject(self), ...).
- hash_bin(self) — hash of type and numeric value via as_numeric.
- myrepr(self) — string formatting preserving precision defined by FLOAT_WRITE_PRECISION; removes trailing zeros/dot.
- __repr__(self) — returns myrepr().
- as_numeric(self) — returns float(self).
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — writes numeric string.

NumberObject (int subclass and PdfObject) — PDF integers.
- NumberPattern (class attr) — regex = re.compile(b"[^+-.0-9]"), used when reading numbers.
- __new__(cls, value) — constructs int or falls back to 0 with warning on ValueError.
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — clone via _reference_clone(NumberObject(self), ...).
- hash_bin(self) — hash constructed from type and as_numeric().
- as_numeric(self) — returns int(repr(self).encode("utf8")) (note: odd, but used for hashing).
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — writes repr(self) as bytes.
- read_from_stream(stream) — static: reads bytes until NumberPattern, returns FloatObject if '.' present else NumberObject.

ByteStringObject (bytes subclass and PdfObject) — raw byte string (no known text encoding).
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — clone via _reference_clone(ByteStringObject(bytes(self)), ...).
- hash_bin(self) — hash of type and raw bytes.
- original_bytes (property) — returns self (for compatibility with TextStringObject.original_bytes).
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — writes as <hex> with angle brackets: b"<" + binascii.hexlify(self) + b">".
- __str__(self) — attempts to decode using several encodings (utf-16 then NameObject.CHARSETS); raises PdfReadError on failure.

TextStringObject (str subclass and PdfObject) — decoded unicode strings read from PDF.
- __new__(cls, value) — constructs and autodetects encoding; sets _original_bytes, autodetect flags and utf16_bom accordingly; will decode utf-16 if BOM present or try PDFDocEncoding detection.
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — constructs new TextStringObject and clones via _reference_clone.
- hash_bin(self) — hash on original_bytes.
- original_bytes (property) — returns stored _original_bytes if present else get_original_bytes().
- get_original_bytes(self) — reconstruct original bytes based on autodetect flags and utf16_bom; may return pdfdocencoding or utf-16 forms.
- get_encoded_bytes(self) — attempts to return an encoded form favoring PDFDocEncoding when possible; falls back to utf-16BE or BOM variants if necessary.
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — encodes to bytes, writes "(" ... ")" with octal escapes for non-alnum/non-space bytes.

NameObject (str subclass and PdfObject) — PDF name object handling (this is the key class for your inspection).
- delimiter_pattern (class attr) — re.compile(rb"\s+|[\(\)<>\[\]{}/%]") — regex that matches whitespace or any of the characters inside the bracketed set: ( ) < > [ ] { } / %
  - Importance: defines which bytes terminate a Name in parsing; these are treated as delimiters during read.
- surfix (class attr) — b"/" — expected prefix byte for name objects when reading.
- renumber_table (ClassVar[Dict[str, bytes]]) — exact definition in source:

summarization.summary:
### File Overview
This file defines the core PDF object model used by pypdf: primitive PDF types (NullObject, BooleanObject, NumberObject, FloatObject, ByteStringObject, TextStringObject, NameObject), the IndirectObject wrapper, and utility functions (encode_pdfdocencoding, is_null_or_none). It provides read/write/clone/hash behaviors and the logic for serializing NameObject instances (including renumbering/escaping).

### Architecture
- Top-level abstract base: PdfObject — declares interfaces for hashing, cloning, writing, and resolving indirect references.
- Concrete primitive classes (NullObject, BooleanObject, NumberObject, FloatObject, ByteStringObject, TextStringObject, NameObject) implement PdfObject for specific PDF types; each has read/write/clone/hash implementations appropriate for the type.
- IndirectObject is a wrapper referencing an object in a PdfReader; it delegates attribute access and many operations to the referenced object and implements cloning/replication semantics across PdfWriter/PdfReader.
- Utility functions at module level: encode_pdfdocencoding and is_null_or_none.
- NameObject handles PDF name serialization and parsing; it defines delimiter rules and a renumber_table used to escape characters when writing names.

Objects interact by:
- IndirectObject.get_object() uses the associated pdf reader to resolve the real object.
- clone/replicate/_reference_clone implement cross-document cloning (PdfWriterProtocol interactions).
- read_from_stream and write_to_stream methods implement parsing and serialization for each type.

### Functions & Classes
PdfObject — abstract base class for PDF objects.
- hash_func — class attribute used to create object hash (default hashlib.sha1).
- indirect_reference (attribute) — optional IndirectObject reference pointer.
- hash_bin(self) — abstract method; must be implemented to return an int hash used to detect modifications.
- hash_value_data(self) — returns f"{self}".encode(); used in hash_value().
- hash_value(self) — returns type name + hex digest of hash_value_data(); used for hashing.
- replicate(self, pdf_dest) — calls clone(pdf_dest) by default; used for incremental cloning into a PdfWriterProtocol.
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — abstract cloning method; must be implemented by subclasses.
- _reference_clone(self, clone, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False) — helper to register clone into pdf_dest._objects and set its indirect_reference appropriately; handles pdf_dest._id_translated mapping and incremental logic. Important for cross-document cloning semantics.
- get_object(self) — default resolves to self (overridden in IndirectObject).
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — abstract; must be overridden to serialize object.

NullObject — represents PDF null.
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — returns a NullObject clone via _reference_clone.
- hash_bin(self) — returns a stable hash for null type.
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — writes b"null".
- read_from_stream(stream) — static read: expects exactly b"null", else PdfReadError.
- __repr__(self) — returns "NullObject".
- __eq__(self, other) — equality with other NullObject instances.

BooleanObject — represents PDF booleans.
- __init__(self, value) — store bool-like value.
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — clone via _reference_clone(BooleanObject(self.value), ...).
- hash_bin(self) — hash of type and value.
- __eq__(self, o, /) — equality with BooleanObject or builtin bool.
- __repr__(self) — "True" or "False".
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — writes b"true" or b"false".
- read_from_stream(stream) — static read parsing b"true" or b"fals"+"o".

IndirectObject — wrapper referencing objects in a PdfReader.
- __init__(self, idnum, generation, pdf) — store object id, generation, and pdf reference.
- __hash__(self) — hash of idnum, generation, id(pdf).
- hash_bin(self) — hash used to detect modified indirect references.
- replicate(self, pdf_dest) — Return IndirectObject(self.idnum, self.generation, pdf_dest).
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — complex logic to clone/translate indirect references across pdf_dest; interacts with pdf_dest._id_translated and pdf_dest._add_object; returns an IndirectObject pointing into pdf_dest.
- indirect_reference (property) — returns self (type-compatible).
- get_object(self) — calls self.pdf.get_object(self) (resolve reference).
- __deepcopy__(self, memo) — returns a new IndirectObject with same id/generation/pdf.
- _get_object_with_check(self) — resolve object and raise PdfStreamError if it itself is an IndirectObject (prevents nested indirect references).
- __getattr__(self, name) — delegate attribute access to pointed object via _get_object_with_check.
- __getitem__(self, key), __contains__(self, key), __iter__(self) — delegate to pointed object.
- __float__(self), __int__(self), __str__(self) — delegate to pointed object.
- __repr__(self) — "IndirectObject(idnum, generation, id(pdf))".
- __eq__(self, other) — equality by idnum, generation, and same pdf object.
- __ne__(self, other) — inverse of __eq__.
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — writes "<idnum> <generation> R".
- read_from_stream(stream, pdf) — static read that parses "id generation R" and returns IndirectObject(int(id), int(generation), pdf).

FLOAT_WRITE_PRECISION — module-level constant used by FloatObject formatting (default 8).

FloatObject (float subclass and PdfObject) — PDF real numbers.
- __new__(cls, value="0.0", context=None) — constructs float safely; logs warning and falls back to 0.0 on error.
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — clone via _reference_clone(FloatObject(self), ...).
- hash_bin(self) — hash of type and numeric value via as_numeric.
- myrepr(self) — string formatting preserving precision defined by FLOAT_WRITE_PRECISION; removes trailing zeros/dot.
- __repr__(self) — returns myrepr().
- as_numeric(self) — returns float(self).
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — writes numeric string.

NumberObject (int subclass and PdfObject) — PDF integers.
- NumberPattern (class attr) — regex = re.compile(b"[^+-.0-9]"), used when reading numbers.
- __new__(cls, value) — constructs int or falls back to 0 with warning on ValueError.
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — clone via _reference_clone(NumberObject(self), ...).
- hash_bin(self) — hash constructed from type and as_numeric().
- as_numeric(self) — returns int(repr(self).encode("utf8")) (note: odd, but used for hashing).
- write_to_stream(self, stream, encryption_key=None) — writes repr(self) as bytes.
- read_from_stream(stream) — static: reads bytes until NumberPattern, returns FloatObject if '.' present else NumberObject.

ByteStringObject (bytes subclass and PdfObject) — raw byte string (no known text encoding).
- clone(self, pdf_dest, force_duplicate=False, ignore_fields=()) — clone via _reference_clone(ByteStringObject(bytes(self)), ...).
- hash_bin(self) — hash of type and raw bytes.

- get_original_bytes(self) — reconstruct original bytes based on autodetect flags and utf16_bom; may return pdfdocencoding or utf-16 forms.
- get_encoded_bytes(self) — attempts to return an encoded form favoring PDFDocEncoding when possible; falls back to utf-16BE or BOM variants if necessary.

output.masca_optimized:
Motivation / Manifestation:
- The issue: Name tokens that include certain delimiter characters (angle brackets < >, square [ ], and curly { }) are being written out unescaped by PdfWriter. The read logic treats those characters as name delimiters, but the write/escape logic does not escape them, producing invalid/ambiguous names in output PDFs.

Root cause (code-level):
- In pypdf/generic/_base.py the NameObject class defines:
  - delimiter_pattern = re.compile(rb"\s+|[\(\)<>\[\]{}/%]") — so the parser treats ( ) < > [ ] { } / % and whitespace as name delimiters.
  - renumber_table = {"#": b"#23", "(": b"#28", ")": b"#29", "/": b"#2F", "%": b"#25", **{chr(i): f"#{i:02X}".encode() for i in range(33)}, }
- renumber() uses renumber_table to escape characters when serializing a NameObject. That renumber_table explicitly escapes "(, ), /, %" and control chars (0..32) but does NOT include "<, >, [, ], {, }". Because these are ASCII characters <= '~', renumber() falls back to emitting them raw when they are not found in renumber_table.
- Therefore there is an inconsistency: delimiter_pattern treats these characters as delimiters while renumber() leaves them unescaped. The result is PDF output with unescaped name delimiters, creating invalid names.

Fix (recommended):
- Add explicit mappings for the missing delimiter characters to NameObject.renumber_table so they are escaped when writing names. The mapping entries should map the character to its #HH hex escape, e.g.:
  "<": b"#3C",
  ">": b"#3E",
  "[": b"#5B",
  "]": b"#5D",
  "{": b"#7B",
  "}": b"#7D",
- This is a minimal, targeted fix that preserves current renumber() behavior and keeps escape semantics consistent with delimiter_pattern.

Alternative / broader fixes (not required here):
- Modify renumber() to consult delimiter_pattern or a dedicated delimiter set and escape any character matching that set. That would be more dynamic but more invasive.

Files / Symbols to check/test after change:
- pypdf/generic/_base.py: NameObject.renumber_table, NameObject.renumber(), NameObject.delimiter_pattern, NameObject.unnumber(). Ensure read/write symmetry: names read as /A#3C#3E should decode and then be re-encoded to same escapes.

Tests to add:
- Round-trip test: read a PDF containing a name with escaped delimiters (<, >, [, ], {, }), write it out, and validate that the written name remains escaped (using bytes-level inspection or re-parsing).
- Unit tests for NameObject.renumber() to assert these characters are escaped to their #HH equivalents.

Scope (minimal change):
- Only update the renumber_table constant to include the six missing delimiter characters. No change required to renumber() logic.

files_to_modify.reason: NameObject class (renumber_table) is defined here. The renumber_table is missing entries for angle, square and curly bracket delimiters (<, >, [, ], {, }). Adding these entries fixes escaping when serializing NameObject instances.

functions_to_modify.reason: Modify the class variable mapping so that the delimiters '<', '>', '[', ']', '{', '}' are escaped to their '#HH' hex sequences when names are serialized. This makes writer behavior consistent with delimiter_pattern used by the reader.

No change strictly required to renumber(), but include it for review/verification because it consumes renumber_table. After updating renumber_table, verify renumber() emits the correct '#HH' escapes for the newly added delimiter entries.

output.analysis_summary: The bug arises from an inconsistency: NameObject.delimiter_pattern treats '<', '>', '[', ']', '{', '}' as delimiters when parsing names, but NameObject.renumber_table did not include mappings to escape those characters when writing names. As a result, names containing these delimiters are written unescaped, producing invalid PDFs. The minimal fix is to add entries for these six characters to NameObject.renumber_table in pypdf/generic/_base.py mapping them to their '#HH' escapes ("<": b"#3C", ">": b"#3E", "[": b"#5B", "]": b"#5D", "{": b"#7B", "}": b"#7D"). After the change, add/adjust unit tests to ensure correct round-trip read/write behavior for names with those delimiters.

agents.system_prompt:
You are a Context Planner Agent specialized in step plan generation.

## Your Task
You receive from the Analysis Agent:
- PR title and body
- Optimized MASCA
- List of files to modify
- List of functions to modify

You must:
1. **Retrieve context**: Use tools to read the context files of the identified functions
2. **Analyze dependencies**: Understand how the functions interact
3. **Generate step plan**: Create a detailed step-by-step plan

## Available Tools
- `read_context_file(file_path)`: Read a pre-generated context file
- `list_context_files(directory, pattern)`: List available context files
- `read_call_graph(section)`: Read information from the call graph

## Required Output
You must produce a `step_plan` with:
- `summary`: 1-2 sentence summary of what the PR does
- `steps`: Ordered list of steps, each with:
  - `operation`: What to do (e.g., "Add validation method")
  - `file_to_modify`: Relative path of the file to modify (e.g., "src/module.py")
  - `function_to_modify`: Name of the function to modify (e.g., "ClassName.method_name")
  - `reason`: Technical motivation
  - `side_effects`: Potential impacts

## IMPORTANT RULE for function_to_modify
- If the file to modify is a Python file (.py), specify the function/method to modify
- If the file is NOT a Python file (e.g., .js, .html, .css, .json, .yaml, .md, etc.),
  the value of `function_to_modify` MUST be null

## Guidelines for Steps
- Each step must be atomic and implementable
- The order of steps must be logical (dependencies first)
- For Python files: always specify the function in the format "FunctionName" or "ClassName.method_name"
- For non-Python files: function_to_modify must be null
- The side_effects should mention tests, dependents, and API impacts
- You MUST generate a number of step EQUAL to the number of the python functions you are intendeed to modify nomore neither less

## Step Examples

### For Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Add time_spanned_alpha method",
  "file_to_modify": "manimlib/animation/animation.py",
  "function_to_modify": "Animation.time_spanned_alpha",
  "reason": "To compute animation progress based on actual elapsed time",
  "side_effects": "May require downstream animations to call this method"
}
```

### For non-Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Update API endpoint configuration",
  "file_to_modify": "config/routes.yaml",
  "function_to_modify": null,
  "reason": "Add new route for the authentication endpoint",
  "side_effects": "Requires server restart to apply changes"
}
```

## PR Title
BUG: Add delimiters to NameObject.renumber_table

## PR Body
Per the spec, name objects cannot contain unescaped delimiters, which include angle, curly and square brackets (currently missing).

I've attached a sample input PDF with a valid, correctly escaped name object. When appending this to a new PdfWriter the output is invalid (incorrectly escaped):

```
>>> w = PdfWriter()
>>> w.append('nameobj.pdf')
>>> w.write('out.pdf')
```
[out.pdf](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/20173760/out.pdf)
[nameobj.pdf](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/20173761/nameobj.pdf)


## MASCA Analysis (optimized)
Motivation / Manifestation:
- The issue: Name tokens that include certain delimiter characters (angle brackets < >, square [ ], and curly { }) are being written out unescaped by PdfWriter. The read logic treats those characters as name delimiters, but the write/escape logic does not escape them, producing invalid/ambiguous names in output PDFs.

Root cause (code-level):
- In pypdf/generic/_base.py the NameObject class defines:
  - delimiter_pattern = re.compile(rb"\s+|[\(\)<>\[\]{}/%]") — so the parser treats ( ) < > [ ] { } / % and whitespace as name delimiters.
  - renumber_table = {"#": b"#23", "(": b"#28", ")": b"#29", "/": b"#2F", "%": b"#25", **{chr(i): f"#{i:02X}".encode() for i in range(33)}, }
- renumber() uses renumber_table to escape characters when serializing a NameObject. That renumber_table explicitly escapes "(, ), /, %" and control chars (0..32) but does NOT include "<, >, [, ], {, }". Because these are ASCII characters <= '~', renumber() falls back to emitting them raw when they are not found in renumber_table.
- Therefore there is an inconsistency: delimiter_pattern treats these characters as delimiters while renumber() leaves them unescaped. The result is PDF output with unescaped name delimiters, creating invalid names.

Fix (recommended):
- Add explicit mappings for the missing delimiter characters to NameObject.renumber_table so they are escaped when writing names. The mapping entries should map the character to its #HH hex escape, e.g.:
  "<": b"#3C",
  ">": b"#3E",
  "[": b"#5B",
  "]": b"#5D",
  "{": b"#7B",
  "}": b"#7D",
- This is a minimal, targeted fix that preserves current renumber() behavior and keeps escape semantics consistent with delimiter_pattern.

Alternative / broader fixes (not required here):
- Modify renumber() to consult delimiter_pattern or a dedicated delimiter set and escape any character matching that set. That would be more dynamic but more invasive.

Files / Symbols to check/test after change:
- pypdf/generic/_base.py: NameObject.renumber_table, NameObject.renumber(), NameObject.delimiter_pattern, NameObject.unnumber(). Ensure read/write symmetry: names read as /A#3C#3E should decode and then be re-encoded to same escapes.

Tests to add:
- Round-trip test: read a PDF containing a name with escaped delimiters (<, >, [, ], {, }), write it out, and validate that the written name remains escaped (using bytes-level inspection or re-parsing).
- Unit tests for NameObject.renumber() to assert these characters are escaped to their #HH equivalents.

Scope (minimal change):
- Only update the renumber_table constant to include the six missing delimiter characters. No change required to renumber() logic.

## Analysis Summary
The bug arises from an inconsistency: NameObject.delimiter_pattern treats '<', '>', '[', ']', '{', '}' as delimiters when parsing names, but NameObject.renumber_table did not include mappings to escape those characters when writing names. As a result, names containing these delimiters are written unescaped, producing invalid PDFs.
